deck: Part one of the Dimagi founding story, told by co-founders Dr. Vikram Kumar and Jonathan Jackson: a chance meeting at MIT, a 15-second decision to join, and a first national medical record project in Zambia that set the company's course.
seo_description: Dimagi co-founders Vikram Kumar and Jonathan Jackson on the company's earliest days, their MIT meeting, and the Zambia medical record project that shaped it.
og_description: The Dimagi founding story, part one: how Vikram Kumar and Jonathan Jackson met at MIT and built a first project in Zambia.
about: In the first part of Dimagi's founding story, co-host Amie Vaccaro talks with co-founder Dr. Vikram Kumar and co-founder and CEO Jonathan Jackson about the company's earliest days. Vikram, a medical student working at the MIT Media Lab, wanted to surface healthcare problems so engineers could solve them at scale, an idea that became Dimagi, a name drawn from the Hindi word for brain. Alongside co-founders Vishwanath and Tarjay Mickelson, the team began developing early work to improve rural healthcare, starting in India with the All India Institute of Medical Sciences.

Jonathan, a 22-year-old MIT computer science graduate searching for fulfilling work, met Vikram through an MIT development ventures club led by a figure named Yost. Within seconds of their first conversation, Jonathan knew he had found his calling and effectively hired himself. Within about a year, he proposed to Vikram that he become CEO, and Vikram supported the quick transition. Three weeks after joining, Jonathan took his first trip to Africa to lead Dimagi's first major project: a PEPFAR-funded national medical record system in Zambia.

That Zambia project shaped Dimagi's enduring approach, including the field-driven user-centered design the team once called designing for the mango tree. It also led to a defining tough decision: when the project's heavy focus on central data collection diverged from Dimagi's frontline-worker-centric vision, the team chose to walk away, giving up half its revenue at the time. The company recovered that revenue within the year and grew faster by better aligning impact with its partners.

pull_quote.text: I think one of the interesting things that I didn't know, it sounds silly in retrospect, but you literally do step off the plane when you land in Africa, there's no jet bridges. And so there was literally a visceral sensation you get when you step off the plane, because it just smells different.
pull_quote.cite: Jonathan Jackson, on his first trip to Africa
